Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 66 actions; a strong score needs about 33 or fewer. You are 2.0x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
I see the gray-bordered objects are diamond-shaped (4-connected diamonds):
- (9,49): boundary [[7, 49], [9, 51], [11, 49], [9, 47]] - diamond centered at (9,49)
- (21,8): boundary [[19, 8], [21, 10], [23, 8], [21, 6]] - diamond centered at (21,8)
- (35,45): boundary [[33, 45], [35, 47], [37, 45], [35, 43]] - diamond centered at (35,45)
- (48,54): boundary [[46, 54], [48, 56], [50, 54], [48, 52]] - diamond centered at (48,54)
These are diamond-shaped markers. Two have orange interiors and two have purple interiors.
Looking at the current state:
- Orange object at (14,28)
- Purple object at (41,49)
The orange object is on a trail that goes from (12,24) through (14,27), (14,28), etc.
The purple object is on a trail that goes from (41,49) through other positions.
Maybe the goal is to move the orange object to one of the orange-interior diamond markers, or the purple object to one of the purple-interior diamond markers.
Let me think about this differently. Looking at the image:
- There's an orange circle on a white trail in the upper part of the board.
- There's a purple circle on a white trail in the lower part of the board.
- There are four diamond markers: two orange and two purple.
Maybe the goal is to move the orange circle to one of the orange diamond markers, and the purple circle to one of the purple diamond markers.
Let me try clicking on the orange object to move it further along the trail. But wait, I already did that and it moved from (12,24) to (14,27) to (14,28). Clicking on (14,28) didn't move it further.
Let me check if the orange object is still at (14,28) or if it moved. Looking at the current state, the orange object is at (14,28).
Wait, I need to re-examine the history. After the reset (turn 80), the orange object was at (12,24). Then:
- Turn 81: Click on (12,24) -> orange moved to (14,27)
- Turn 82: Click on (14,27) -> orange moved to (14,28)
- Turn 83: Click on (14,28) -> orange stayed at (14,28)
- Turn 84: Click on (41,49) -> orange stayed at (14,28)
- Turn 85: Click on (14,25) -> orange stayed at (14,28)
So clicking on the orange object moves it one step along the trail. But after two steps, it stopped moving. This suggests the trail has a specific path and the orange object can only move a certain number of steps.
Wait, looking at the earlier history (before the reset), I see:
- Turn 21: Click on (12,24) -> orange moved to (9,30)
- Turn 22: Click on (9,30) -> orange moved to (8,32)
- Turn 28: Click on (41,49) -> orange moved to (18,38)
- Turn 29: Click on (18,38) -> orange moved to (11,34)
- Turn 32: Click on (41,49) -> orange moved to (20,44)
- Turn 33: Click on (20,44) -> orange moved to (13,42)
- Turn 34: Click on (13,42) -> orange moved to (11,41)
So clicking on the orange object moves it one step along the trail. But clicking on the purple object (41,49) also moves the orange object! This is interesting.
Let me think about this:
- Clicking on the orange object moves it one step along its trail.
- Clicking on the purple object also moves the orange object, but to a different position.
Maybe the purple object acts as a "teleport" or "jump" point for the orange object. Or maybe clicking on the purple object moves the orange object along a different trail.
Actually, looking more carefully:
- Turn 28: Click on (41,49) [purple] -> orange moved from (8,32) to (18,38)
- Turn 32: Click on (41,49) [purple] -> orange moved from (11,41) to (20,44)
So clicking on the purple object moves the orange object to a new position. This suggests the purple object is a "jump" point that teleports the orange object to a different part of the trail.
Let me try clicking on the purple object again to see if it moves the orange object.
